problem_addressed: The urgent need for quantitative evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) as agents in interactive environments to assess their reasoning and decision-making abilities.
core_claim: Top commercial LLMs demonstrate strong agent capabilities while open-source models show significant performance gaps, with poor long-term reasoning, decision-making, and instruction following identified as main obstacles.
key_contributions: AGENTBENCH: A multi-dimensional benchmark consisting of 8 distinct environments for evaluating LLM-as-Agent capabilities
Extensive testing of 29 API-based and open-source LLMs across these environments
Identification of typical failure reasons in environments and LLMs, revealing key obstacles to usable LLM agents
novelty: Creation of the first comprehensive benchmark that holistically evaluates LLMs across multiple agent-like environments (Operating System, Database, Knowledge, Graph, Digital Card Game, Lateral Thinking Puzzle, House-holding, Web) rather than isolated tasks.
method_summary: AGENTBENCH creates 8 distinct interactive environments to test LLM agents, then evaluates 29 API-based and open-source LLMs across these environments to assess reasoning and decision-making abilities.
method_components: Eight distinct environments: Operating System, Database, Knowledge, Graph, Digital Card Game, Lateral Thinking Puzzle, House-holding, Web
Evaluation of 29 API-based and open-source LLMs across these environments
key_innovation: Multi-dimensional benchmark design that holistically evaluates LLM agent capabilities across diverse interactive environments rather than isolated tasks.
assumptions: The 8 environments adequately represent real-world agent tasks
LLMs can meaningfully interact with these environments
Performance across environments correlates with practical agent usability
stated_limitations: Significant performance disparity between top commercial LLMs and many OSS competitors (≤70B)
They identify typical reasons for failures in environments and LLMs
unstated_weaknesses: No explicit focus on attention mechanism efficiency metrics
Limited information about diversity and complexity of benchmark environments
Potential bias toward models with larger parameter counts
Unclear how environments specifically test attention mechanisms
threats_to_validity: Benchmark may not cover scenarios where efficient attention mechanisms would be most beneficial
Evaluation might not isolate attention mechanism performance from other model components
Environments might favor certain types of reasoning that don't directly test attention efficiency
Potential bias toward models with more training data
open_questions: How do different attention mechanisms perform across the benchmark environments?
What is the relationship between attention efficiency and overall agent performance?
Are there specific environments where efficient attention mechanisms provide the most benefit?
How does the computational cost of attention mechanisms relate to task performance?
future_work: Expanding the benchmark to include more diverse environments
Developing more specialized benchmarks for specific aspects of agent performance
Investigating the failure cases in more detail
gaps_this_enables: A framework for evaluating how attention mechanism improvements translate to agent performance
Identification of specific scenarios where efficient attention would be most beneficial
A baseline for comparing different attention mechanisms in agent contexts
Insights into the trade-offs between attention efficiency and task performance
relevance_to_topic: This paper provides a benchmark framework for evaluating how different attention mechanisms perform in agent scenarios, which could inform the development of more efficient attention mechanisms.
limitations_stated: Significant performance disparity between top commercial LLMs and many OSS competitors (≤70B)
They identify typical reasons for failures in environments and LLMs
title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
